---
title: "The silence after the light"
---

[← Back to research](../../research.qmd){.back-link}

[co-first author · preprint · bioRxiv, 2026]{.paper-meta}

Most optogenetic silencers, like the light-gated chloride channel GtACR1, only silence for as
long as the light stays on: the neuron switches back on the moment you switch the light off.
That is a problem for behavior, where continuous actuating light can itself confound the very
thing you are trying to measure.

![](graphical-abstract.png){.graphical-abstract style="max-width:760px" fig-alt="Graphical abstract: a brief green light pulse gives GtACR1 transient inhibition but OPN3, a bistable Gi/o GPCR, prolonged inhibition; OPN3 silencing is tested in Drosophila memory and locomotion assays"}

[Graphical abstract from Lee N.M., Mai Y., Dalberg L., Anns J.C., Suresh D.D., Zhang Z. &amp;
Claridge-Chang A. (2026). *Brief illumination of an optoGPCR elicits prolonged inhibition of
Drosophila behavior.* **bioRxiv**.]{.figure-credit style="max-width:760px"}

## The tool: a light-triggered, self-sustaining silencer (OPN3)

This paper characterizes a different kind of tool. **OPN3** — Opsin3 from the mosquito
*Anopheles stephensi* — is a *bistable* Gi/o-coupled GPCR: a single brief flash of green light
sets off an intracellular signaling cascade that keeps the neuron inhibited long after the
light is gone. We screened three optoGPCRs in adult *Drosophila*; OPN3 came out clearly ahead
and, to our knowledge, is the **first GPCR opsin shown to inhibit behavior in adult
*Drosophila***. A pulse as short as 5 seconds silenced behavior for *minutes*, and one such
pulse was enough to erase aversive olfactory learning across a one-minute training epoch.
Benchmarked head-to-head against GtACR1 in walking and memory assays, OPN3's silencing potency
was comparable across most neuron classes tested — but with substantially lower developmental
toxicity and better viability.

Because the silencing outlasts the light, you can flash once and then record in the dark. That
decoupling matters most in experiments where the actuating light is itself a confound: it adds
cumulative light exposure over long sessions, clashes with concurrent calcium imaging, and
brings heating and phototoxicity. A clear example is vision — in work on the visual system,
the light you would need to keep a conventional silencer switched on also stimulates the very
photoreceptors and circuits you are studying. With OPN3 you deliver one brief pulse, then
observe freely in darkness, so the tool that silences the neuron no longer interferes with what
you are trying to see.

## The second finding: developmental retinal (ATR) is a lever on performance

Every opsin is only half a tool on its own. The protein is an apoprotein: it needs a small
light-absorbing molecule, the chromophore retinal, bound inside it before it can respond to
light at all. *Drosophila* don't make enough of it on their own, so optogenetics in flies almost
always means feeding them all-trans-retinal (ATR) — and by convention that feeding starts in
*adulthood*, once the neurons you want to control are already wired up.

We asked whether *when* the retinal is available matters. Rearing flies on ATR-supplemented food
throughout development, rather than only in adulthood, improved *both* survival to adulthood
*and* how strongly the light silenced neurons — and it did so for the chloride channel GtACR1 as
well as for OPN3. That the same simple change helped two mechanistically unrelated tools points at
something upstream of either one: developmental retinal availability is a **lever on optogenetic
performance itself**, not just a housekeeping detail. Practically, it is a cheap tweak to how flies
are reared that buys both healthier animals and a stronger effect — worth building into the
protocol rather than treating as an afterthought.

To learn more: read the preprint on
[bioRxiv](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898/2026.05.26.727874v1) —
[doi:10.64898/2026.05.26.727874](https://doi.org/10.64898/2026.05.26.727874).

[]{.section-rule}
